chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Software Security. (3-0). Credit 3. Basic principles of design and implementation of defect-free software, code reviews including tool-assisted review by static and dynamic analysis, risk analysis and management and methods for software security testing. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331; or approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Phone: (979) 458-6850
Preferred Contact Method: By email
Webpage: https://marcusbotacin.github.io/

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331 or approval of the instructor.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

*   list the first principles of security and explain why each is important to security and how it enables the development of security mechanisms that can implement desired security policies.
*   identify specific principles that have been violated in common security failures.
*   identify appropriate design principles to apply in each software development scenario.
*   explain the interaction between security and system usability and importance of human-computer interfaces to system usability.
*   explain the importance of secure software and the programming practices, development processes, and methodologies that lead to secure software.
*   explain techniques for specifying program behavior, the classes of well-known defects, and how they manifest themselves in various languages.
*   perform penetration testing on previously unknown software.
*   analyze existing source code for functional correctness.
*   analyze software for defects using industry standard tools.
*   develop test cases that demonstrate the existence of defects.
*   develop defect-free software components that satisfy their functional requirements.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Optional
Provided by the Instructor
Notes:
No textbook is required. All materials will be posted on the class website (Canvas). Students should expect to run security tools on their own machines. This course follows the minimum device requirements established by the College of Engineering (https://engineering.tamu.edu/academics/byod/devices/index.html).

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The grading is composed of:
*   Vulnerabilities PoC* (Individual): 50%
*   First project** (*Groups of 4 students): 25%
*   Final project*** (*Groups of 4 students): 25%
*   *Group size might be adjusted to accommodate a large number of students enrolled in the course, at the instructor's discretion. Ask for permission.
*   Independent-Study Reports**** (optional, individual): +10%
*   No written exam, no quiz.
Total: 100%

*Vulnerabilities PoC
*   Students are required to deliver PoCs for the vulnerabilities explained in class by the instructor. PoCs should be an end-to-end exploit of the sample applications provided by the instructor.
*   The PoC is considered successful if it meets all the requirements below:
    *   The student delivers a source code for the PoC.
    *   The student delivers a video demonstrating the exploitation at the student machine.
    *   The exploitation can be reproduced by the instructor/TA.

**First Project
*   Each group should:
    *   Students should identify and exploit an exploitable Web vulnerability in a Web-based project available on GitHub.
    *   The vulnerability should not have been reported before in any sense (i.e., no known CVEs)
    *   The students should responsibly disclose the vulnerabilities to the project creators according to the instructions provided in the lecture on the topic. Projects without proper disclosure will not be considered.
    *   The exploitation should be done locally (e.g., in Docker) and not in a live system. Exploitation attempts causing actual harm will not be allowed.
    *   The students should deliver a PoC and a video for the exploitation, as with the traditional PoC assignments.
    *   The students should deliver a presentation report in class on the final project dates.

***Final Project
*   Each group should:
    *   Students should identify and exploit an exploitable non-Web vulnerability in a real application,
    *   Web applications are not valid for the final project.
    *   The same rules as for the First project apply.

****Independent Self-Study
*   Students are encouraged to deliver a 1-page summary of known vulnerabilities from the literature, upon the instructor's suggestion. The evaluation is as follows:
    *   Identifying pre-conditions: 10%
    *   Technical Explanation: 50%
    *   Remediation Explanation: 30%
    *   Contextualization with other class topics: 10%
*   Topics:
    *   The topics will include vulnerabilities pointed out by the instructor in Canvas and all final project presentations by all teams.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Extensions/make-ups can be given 12 hours prior to the deadline upon written/in-person justification request. 20min will be given as a grace period. Otherwise, 50% off from the credit you got for that submission. Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: The use of AI support is allowed if any assignment delivered is followed by the delivery of all AI interactions (prompts, responses, chatbot session links) to the most complete extent possible to allow the instructor to evaluate the student rationale to solve the problem.
